coredata.dc:title: A squaraine-based fluorescence turn on chemosensor with ICT character for highly selective and sensitive detection of Al3+ in aqueous media and its application in living cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jianqi
Li, Yuanfang
Shen, Shen
Yan, Qiuming
Xia, Guomin
Wang, Hongming
coredata.dc:description: A novel and simple squaraine-based fluorescent chemosensor SQ-BH bearing the benzoylhydrazine moiety was developed for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Al3+ in methanol-water mixture. The chemosensing behaviors of SQ-BH and its binding interaction with Al3+ were explored by various spectroscopic analyses. The reversibility of Al3+ recognition process was investigated using EDTA. The results of experiments and DFT/TDDFT calculations revealed that the chemosensor SQ-BH obeyed a turn on mechanism which was associated with the inhibited photoinduced electron transfer (PET), the enhanced intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and the activated chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF). Furthermore, the fluorescent chemosensor SQ-BH whose excellent biocompatibility was confirmed by a standard MTT assay could be used to detect Al3+ in living cells, indicating its potential application value in biological fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Squaraine
Fluorescent chemosensor
Aluminum
Mechanism
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoproperties of SQ-BH towards various metal ions
Stoichiometry and binding property of SQ-BH towards Al3+
Theoretical calculation for SQ-BH and SQ-BH-Al3+ complex
Cell toxicity of SQ-BH and its bioimaging with Al3+
Comparison with other chemosensor for Al3+

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21363014 , 21103082 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province of China (No. 20142BCB23003 , 20143ACB21021 ), and the Key Science and Technology Program of Education Department of Jiangxi Province (No. GJJ170945 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of sensor SQ-BH (10 μM) in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0) in the absence and presence of 5.0 eq. of various metal ions (Al3+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+) (λex = 420 nm). Inset: pictures of the fluorescence corresponding to sensor SQ-BH and SQ-BH + Al3+ (λex = 365 nm).

UV–Vis absorption spectra of sensor SQ-BH (10 μM) in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0) in the absence and presence of 4.0 eq. of various metal ions (Al3+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+).

Change ratio (F/F0) of fluorescence intensity at 505 nm of sensor SQ-BH (10 μM) in the presence of 5.0 eq. of Al3+ with 5.0 eq. of various metal ions in MeOH-H2O (9: 1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0) (λex = 420 nm). (1) Al3+; (2) Zn2+; (3) Cd2+; (4) Cu2+; (5) Co2+; (6) Ni2+; (7) Mn2+; (8) Fe2+; (9) Pb2+; (10) Hg2+; (11) Fe3+; (12) Cr3+; (13) Mg2+; (14) Ca2+; (15) Ba2+; (16) Ag+; (17) Na+; (18) K+.

Job plot for the determination of the stoichiometry of sensor SQ-BH and Al3+ in the complex. The sum of the total concentration of SQ-BH and Al3+ remained constant (20 μM) and the fluorescence intensity at 505 nm was plotted as a function of the molar ratio of Al3+ (λex = 420 nm).

Fluorescence spectral changes of sensor SQ-BH (10 μM) with addition of different amount of Al3+ (0–34 μM). Inset: The corresponding plots of fluorescence intensity at indicated wavelengths versus different concentration of Al3+.

Partial 1H NMR spectra (400 MHz) of sensor SQ-BH (10 mM) in DMSO‑d
6 : (a) free SQ-BH; (b) SQ-BH + 0.2 eq. of Al3+; (c) SQ-BH + 0.5 eq. of Al3+; (d) SQ-BH + 1 eq. of Al3+; (e) SQ-BH + 2.0 eq. of Al3+.
Fluorescence recognition profile of SQ–BH (10 μM) towards Al3+ in the presence of EDTA disodium in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0) (λex = 420 nm).
Optimized geometric structures and frontier molecular orbitals energy levels (units are ev) of SQ-BH (left) and SQ-BH-Al3+ complex (right).
Cell viability values (%) estimated by MTT proliferation test versus concentrations of SQ-BH after 12 and 24 h incubations at 37 °C in MeOH/H2O.

Confocal fluorescence and bright-field images of HUVEC cells. a. cells supplemented with 10 μM SQ-BH in growth medium for 30 min; b. confocal fluorescence image of cells incubated with SQ-BH (10 μM) and Al(NO3)3 (30 μM) for another 30 min; c. bright-field image of cells shown in panel b; d. overlay image of panels b and c (λex = 405 nm).
The synthetic route of sensor SQ-BH.
Comparison of SQ-BH with some reported chemosensors for the detection of Al3+.

head.ce:title: A squaraine-based fluorescence turn on chemosensor with ICT character for highly selective and sensitive detection of Al3+ in aqueous media and its application in living cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Jiujiang University, Jiujiang, Jiangxi 332005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Jiujiang University, Jiujiang, Jiangxi 332005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang, Jiangxi 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang, Jiangxi 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of pharmacy, Nanchang University, Nanchang, Jiangxi 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of pharmacy, Nanchang University, Nanchang, Jiangxi 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and simple squaraine-based fluorescent chemosensor SQ-BH bearing the benzoylhydrazine moiety was developed for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Al3+ in methanol-water mixture. The chemosensing behaviors of SQ-BH and its binding interaction with Al3+ were explored by various spectroscopic analyses. The reversibility of Al3+ recognition process was investigated using EDTA. The results of experiments and DFT/TDDFT calculations revealed that the chemosensor SQ-BH obeyed a turn on mechanism which was associated with the inhibited photoinduced electron transfer (PET), the enhanced intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and the activated chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF). Furthermore, the fluorescent chemosensor SQ-BH whose excellent biocompatibility was confirmed by a standard MTT assay could be used to detect Al3+ in living cells, indicating its potential application value in biological fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Aluminum as the most abundant metallic element in the lithosphere is extensively dispersed in the environment and closely related to modern life [1,2]. Nevertheless, free Al3+ resulting from acidic rain and human activities can pose a severe threat to biospheres and human health [3,4]. For instance, an excess of aluminum can lead to bone softening, bladder cancer, Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease, and so on [5,6]. Therefore, the detection of Al3+ in the natural environment and ecosystem are of great significance [7–10]. Some conventional methods such as atomic emission spectroscopy [11], ICP mass spectrometry [12], chromatography [13] and electrochemical methods [14] have been reported for the detection of aluminum. However, these techniques are generally arduous and expensive. In recent years, fluorescent chemosensors have received considerable attention due to their rapid, convenient, and sensitive sensing chemical and biological species like cations, anions, amino acids and proteins [15–21]. Nevertheless, it still remains a challenge to develop Al3+-specific fluorescence chemosensor owing to the lack of spectroscopic characteristics, strong hydration and poor coordination abilities of Al3+ [22–24]. Being a hard acid, Al3+ prefers a coordination sphere containing N and O as hard-base donor sites [7,24–26]. Therefore, many oxygen- and nitrogen-rich fluorophores including imidazoline [27], naphthalene [28], rhodamine [29,30], boron dipyrromethene difluoride (BODIPY) [31], pyrenes [32], coumarin [33], and salicylaldehyde [34] etc. have been developed for the detection of Al3+, whose sensing mechanism are mainly based on the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [1,34], intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [24,25], chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) [7,8], excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) [9,26,35], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [36], aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE) [7,22,23,34], and so on. But some of them still suffer from tedious synthetic procedures, harmful organic solvents, high cost and poor water solubility.

Squaraine dyes which are usually characterized by large molar absorption coefficient and strong solution fluorescence [37,38] are attractive as chemosensors due to their excellent optical properties and significant structural features [38–41]. More importantly, squaraine dyes possess potential oxygen and nitrogen donor sites favored by Al3+, which have spurred us to design and synthesize a squaraine-based fluorescence chemosensor SQ for Al3+ [39]. As far as we know, it was the first time that a squaraine scaffold was used for chemosensing Al3+. SQ exhibited a prominent fluorescence enhancement ascribed to the inhibition of CN isomerization (cis-trans) and the activation of chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) for highly sensitive detecting Al3+, Zn2+ and Cd2+ in an order of Al3+ to Zn2+ to Cd2+ due to both the differences in their respective binding modes and different stabilities of metal complexes in ethanol-water buffer solution. However, further expanding applications and improving certain analytical characteristics such as sensitivity, selectivity and even exploring new sensing mechanism promote us to develop new squaraine-based fluorescent sensors of Al3+. Herein, we report a newly synthesized ‘turn on’ fluorescence chemosensor SQ-BH which contains squaraine scaffold and is characterized by ICT feature. Interestingly, SQ-BH not only can be used for the detection of Al3+ in aqueous media with high sensitivity and selectivity but also for imaging and monitoring Al3+ in living cells. The experimental and theoretical results indicated that the sensing mechanism of chemosensor SQ-BH was concerned with a large CHEF accompanying the inhibited PET and an enhanced ICT.

3, 4-dihydroxy-3-cyclobutene-1, 2-dione (squaric acid) and benzoylhydrazine were obtained from Energy-Chemical (China). HUVEC (human umbilical vein endothelial cell) cell line was provided by Xiangya School of Medicine, CSU (China). Unless otherwise stated, all other chemicals and solvents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. The stock solutions of various metal cations and anions were prepared from their chloride/nitrate salts and sodium salts dissolved by fresh deionized water, respectively.

Semisquaraine as the starting material was synthesized according to a literature procedure [42] and all the reactions were conducted under the atmosphere of nitrogen. The synthetic route of sensor SQ-BH was shown in Scheme 1 .

Semisquaraine (301 mg, 1.0 mmol) and benzoylhydrazine (163 mg, 1.2 mmol) were suspended in 20 mL of distilled ethanol in a 50 mL three-necked flask. The mixture was stirred and brought to reflux at 80 °C for 12 h. Then, the resultant precipitate was filtered and further purified by recrystallization from ethanol to give light pink crystals SQ-BH (330 mg, 78.8% yield); 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ(ppm), 11.57 (d, 1H, J = 0.65 Hz), 7.87 (d, 2H, J = 7.76 Hz), 7.85–7.78 (m, 2H), 7.53 (t, 2H, J = 6.88 Hz), 7.59 (d, 1H, J = 7.02 Hz), 7.33–6.60 (m, 2H), 5.93–5.59 (1H, m), 3.37 (s, 4H), 1.44 (d, 4H, J = 1.00 Hz), 1.28 (d, 4H, J = 6.74 Hz), 0.87 (t, 6H, J = 5.96 Hz); 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ(ppm), 195.88, 166.6, 163.77, 133.35, 132.51, 130.87, 129.25, 129.17, 128.06, 127.61, 127.45, 51.14, 29.17, 19.94, 14.21 ppm; Anal. Calcd for SQ-BH: C, 71.60; H, 6.92; N, 10.02. Found: C, 71.66; H, 6.87; N, 10.01. ESI-MS: Calcd. for C25H29N3O3 ([SQ-BH + 2H2O + CH3OH]−):487.2; Found, 487.3.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were collected on a Lambda 750 UV/Vis/NIR Spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, the United State) and an F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. A stock solution of SQ-BH (2.0 mM) was prepared in DMSO and stock solutions of various guest cations (2.0 mM, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Na+, K+) were prepared in deionized water. And stock solutions of common anions (2.0 mM, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, AcO−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, SCN−, S2−, SO4 2−, HSO4 −, ClO4 −) were also prepared in deionized water. Before spectroscopic measurements, test solutions were freshly prepared in HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0, MeOH-H2O = 9:1 (v/v)) by mixing 15 μL stock solution of SQ-BH and an appropriate aliquot of each metal ion stock into cuvettes with the final volume of 3.0 mL respectively. Al3+ was detected by adding different aliquot of stock solution of Al3+ to SQ-BH (10 μM) solution. All UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature after the addition of determinand for a few seconds. The fluorescence spectra were acquired from 430 to 630 nm with the excitation wavelength set as 420 nm. The excitation and emission slit widths were set as 5 and 10 nm, respectively. Spectra of 1H NMR and 13C NMR (TMS as an internal reference) were obtained on a Bruker Avance III 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker Inc., Switzerland). Elemental analysis was carried on an Elementar vario EL III (ELEMENTAR Inc., Germany). Mass spectra were measured on a triple quadrupole mass spectrometer (6430 QqQLC/MS system; Agilent Technologies, the United State). Cytotoxicity of SQ-BH was tested by a standard methylthiazolyltetrazolium (MTT) assay in HUVEC cell lines and cells were imaged by a confocal LSM710 laser-scanning microscope with 405 nm excitation (ZEISS Inc., Germany).

The HUVEC cells growing in RPMI-1640 medium were supplemented with 10% FBS (fetal bovine serum), 1% penicillin-streptomycin and 5% CO2 at 37 °C. After the addition of SQ-BH with the final concentration changing from 0 to 50 μM for 24 h, the combined MTT (20 μL, 5 mg/mL)/RPMI-1640 culture media (100 μL) was added into each well of a 96-well culture plate and incubated for another 3 h. Then, a microplate reader (RT6100, Rayto, China) was used to measure the absorbance at 570 nm of each well. The cell viability was represented by the optical density ratio of the treatment to control.

The HUVEC cells which were passaged in a confocal dish and grew in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 at 37 °C were used when they could adhere and reach an appropriate density. The adherent cells were incubated with 10 μM SQ-BH (obtained by 0.5 mM SQ-BH in MeOH diluted in PBS) at room temperature for 30 min. After being washed with PBS solution three times to remove the remaining SQ-BH, the stained cells were mounted onto a glass slide and imaged by confocal microscopy. Next, the living cell imaging was conducted again after the cells stained with 10 μM SQ-BH were incubated with 30 μM Al(NO3)3 at room temperature for another 30 min.

All calculations were implemented on the Gaussian 09 package [43]. Geometry optimizations involving SQ-BH on the ground state (S0) and the excited state (S1) were performed at the density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) levels, respectively. The B3LYP [44] hybrid functional along with the 6-311G (d,p) basis set were employed by all atoms. In addition, the absorption and emission spectra in relation to SQ-BH were also explored by the same methods. Since the spectroscopic experiments were conducted in methanol-water, the polarizable continuum model (PCM) [45] using methanol as solvent was applied to the whole theoretical calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoproperties of SQ-BH towards various metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the fluorescence intensity of a sensor responding to certain metal ion heavily depends on the property of the solvent used. As shown in Fig. S4a, the present system exhibited the fluorescence emission maximum in methanol among all the solvents tested including water. Therefore, all the measurements were conducted in methanol. Considering the practicality of SQ-BH as a sensor, we also investigated the change of fluorescence intensity of SQ-BH-Al3+ complex in the mixture of methanol and water in varying proportions. It was found that there was a great decrease in the fluorescence intensity for the present sensing system when the volume fraction of H2O is >10%, and the fluorescence emission peak intensity in 10% water mixed with methanol was about 70% of that in pure methanol (Fig. S4b). After all these comprehensive consideration, the further Al titration experiments were performed in 9:1 (v/v) methanol water mixture.

The fluorometric behavior of SQ-BH (10 μM) was investigated in the absence and presence of various metal ions (50 μM) in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0) at ambient temperature (Fig. 1 ). SQ-BH alone showed a very weak and broad emission band centered around 467 nm on excitation at 420 nm. With the addition of 5.0 eq. of Al3+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+, respectively, SQ-BH was almost silent to most of them except Zn2+, to which SQ-BH responded with a weak fluorescence enhancement. In the case of Al3+, however, SQ-BH exhibited a remarkable about 123 fold fluorescence emission enhancement accompanied by a red shift of 38 nm from 467 to 505 nm. Simultaneously, illuminated with a UV lamp (365 nm), the blue-green fluorescence could be easily visualized by naked eye (Inset of Fig. 1). It is generally known that imine is poorly fluorescent partially due to isomerization of the CN bond in the excited state and in part due to the PET process in which the lone pair electrons can be freely transferred from the nitrogen atom of imine moiety to the excited fluorophore [39,46–48]. Therefore, the observed turn on fluorescence might be ascribed to the formation of a rigid system upon complexation with Al3+, which restrained the electron donating ability of the nitrogen atom of the imine moiety and caused the highly effective chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) effect [8,34,39]. While the appearance of a new red shifted emission band suggested an extended conjugated π system and an enhanced ICT effect [49–51].

Similarly, the colorimetric sensing property of SQ-BH was also explored in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0). With the addition of 4.0 eq. of various metal ions, the absorption spectra of SQ-BH (10 μM) were presented in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2, the free SQ-BH exhibited a strong absorbance band at around 397 nm and its spectral changes resulting from the addition of various metal ions could be broadly categorized into four types. Firstly, most of metal ions such as Mn2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Na+ and K+ induced no obvious spectral changes with the exception that the intensity of absorption peak at 397 nm mildly decreased while the absorption at around 420–480 nm slightly increased for a few metal ions among of them. Secondly, Fe2+, Hg2+ and Fe3+, especially Fe2+, created an obvious absorption band centered around 446 nm, meanwhile, the original absorption band centered around 397 nm were greatly diminished and blue shifted to a different extent, respectively. Thirdly, some transition metal ions including Zn2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co2+ and Cd2+ gave rise to a new strong absorbance band centered around 450 nm with the original absorption band centered around 397 nm dramatically decreased and blue shifted to ca. 380 nm, respectively. And finally, Al3+ stood out with the unique changes towards the absorption spectra of SQ-BH. With the addition of Al3+, a new red shifted absorbance band centered at 442 nm appeared clearly while the original absorption band centered at 397 nm had been entirely disappeared, indicating not only the formation of SQ-BH-Al
3+ complexes but also the highest sensitivity and the strongest binding ability of SQ-BH towards Al3+ over other metal ions.

To further test the selectivity of SQ-BH as Al3+-specific fluorescence chemosensor, competitive fluorescence titration experiments were implemented in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0). On the one hand, SQ-BH (10 μM) was titrated with 5.0 eq. of Al3+ in the presence of 5.0 eq. of other metal ions (Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+). As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence enhancement resulting from Al3+ was almost not interfered with most of metal ions except Cu2+ and Fe2+, which were the well-known strong quenchers of fluorescence due to their paramagnetism [8,46] and inhibited about 90% and 75% of the original fluorescence intensity induced by Al3+, respectively. On the other, under the test conditions similar to that of metal ions, interfering effects of some common anions such as F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, AcO−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, SCN−, S2−, SO4 2−, HSO4 − and ClO4 − were also investigated, and no obvious interference on the determination of Al3+ was observed (Fig. S5). The results indicated that SQ-BH possessed the excellent selectivity and specificity to Al3+ over most other competing metal ions and common coexisting anions and could work as a promising selective fluorescent chemosensor for Al3+.

To further investigate the practical applicability of sensor SQ-BH, the effect of pH on the fluorescence response of SQ-BH towards Al3+ was examined in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution since pH of the surroundings plays an important role in fluorescence detection. The fluorescence intensity of SQ-BH (10 μM) in the absence and presence of 5.0 eq. of Al3+ were measured at various pH adjusted by HEPES buffer. The results showed that the free SQ-BH (10 μM) exhibited very week fluorescence at the stated emission wavelength (505 nm) in the pH range of 2–12. However, in the presence of 5.0 eq. of Al3+, the different fluorescence responses of SQ-BH to Al3+ were observed at different pH ranges (Fig. S6). At a pH below 3, the fluorescence intensity of SQ-BH-Al3+ complex in the solution was quite weak because the Schiff base nitrogen atom was protonated and inhibited the chelation of Al3+ with SQ-BH. When the pH value was higher than 10, the fluorescence intensity of SQ-BH-Al3+ complex in the solution decreased dramatically possibly due to the produced Al(OH)3 or Al(OH)4 − species at strong alkaline conditions. Therefore, SQ-BH could efficiently response to Al3+ at pH 3–10 with the fluorescence emission maximum at pH 7, indicating that sensor SQ could be applied in the environmentally and biochemically relevant pH ranges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stoichiometry and binding property of SQ-BH towards Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to deep understand the chemosensing properties of SQ-BH towards Al3+, fluorescence and UV–Vis titration experiments in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0) were conducted to quantitatively evaluate the binding affinity of SQ-BH for Al3+, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 , a Job plot obtained from emission data indicated a 1:1 stoichiometric complexation of SQ-BH with Al3+, which was further confirmed by the peak at m/z 713.3 appearing in the ESI-MS, (Fig. S7), assignable to [SQ-BH + Al3+ + 2CH3OH + 3NO3 − + OH−]−. Then the changes in the fluorescence spectra of SQ-BH upon treatment with Al3+ were measured in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0), as shown in Fig. 5 . Significantly, with the gradual addition of Al3+, unlike the common “turn on” fluorescence resulting from the only inhibited PET, the fluorescence emission peak was red shifted from 467 to 505 nm and the emission intensity at 505 nm did not increase remarkably in a direct way. Instead, it increased gently first then abruptly after the addition of 0.6 eq. of Al3+ (Inset of Fig. 5), which obviously displayed the ICT induced fluorescence characteristics since ICT based fluorescence chemosensor exhibited changes both in emission intensity and emission wavelength whereas those functioning through PET generally only exhibited changes in emission intensity with little even no spectral shift [38,48]. Moreover, the red shift in emission indicated that the coordination of Al3+ to SQ-BH promoted the ICT process of the fluorophore upon excitation. However, in view of the dramatic difference between the emission intensity at 505 nm and that at 467 nm after the addition of Al3+, we speculated that the binding of Al3+ to SQ-BH suppressed the PET process from Schiff base nitrogen atom to benzoyl unit and activate the CHEE process, thus causing a significant enhancement of the emission. Therefore, both the inhibition of PET and the activation of CHEE induced by Al3+ were responsible for fluorescence enhancement while the red shift of emission was attributed to the enhanced ICT. Based on the results of the fluorescence titration shown in Fig. 5, the association constants for SQ-BH-Al3+ in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0) was determined as 1.80 × 105 M−1 by a modified Hill's equation [49] (Fig. S8). In addition, the detection limit for Al3+ was calculated from the calibration curve, namely, from the slope of the curve (S) and the standard deviation (σ) of the blank sample, the detection limit of this fluorescent method was estimated to be 1.91 × 10−8 M using the equation of 3σ/S [10] (Fig. S9), which was quite lower than the WHO recommendation level (7.41 × 10−6 M) for drinking water. In the same time, the calibration curve showed that there was a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.99488) between the fluorescence intensity and Al3+ concentration in the range of 6–16 μM. Obviously, SQ-BH was applicable to the detection of subnanomolar concentrations of Al3+, which belongs to the range found in most environmental and biological systems. As to the change in the absorption spectra of SQ-BH, with the gradual addition of Al3+, the disappearance of absorption peak at 397 nm and the appearance of a new red shifted absorption peak at 442 nm with the concomitant observation of a distinct isosbestic point at about 415 nm indicated the formation of a new complex between SQ-BH and Al3+ (Fig. S10). Furthermore, the red shift change in UV–Vis spectra due to the ICT effect was more obvious than that in fluorescence spectra. Similarly, according to UV–Vis titration results, the association constants and the detection limit for Al3+ were determined to be 7.50 × 105 M−1 (Fig. S11) and 2.29 × 10−7 M (Fig. S12), respectively, and the calibration curve exhibited a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.99031) between the absorbance and Al3+ concentration in the range of 0–18 μM.

The binding mode of SQ-BH with Al3+ was further investigated by 1H NMR titration experiments. The chemical shifts of the protons in SQ-BH before and after the addition of different amounts of Al3+ were depicted in Fig. 6 . With the addition of Al3+, the proton signals Ha in the nitrogen atom of imine moiety at 11.57 ppm almost completely disappeared and the protons signals in the two benzene rings were more complicated (the specific details could be found in Fig. S13), indicating that the nitrogen atom of imine moiety, the carbonyl oxygen atom of squaraine ring and the oxygen atom of benzoyl were involved in Al3+ coordination. Meanwhile, the proton signals for NHCO at 5.69 ppm (Hb) was dramatically downfield shifted by 1.06 to 6.75 ppm (Hb′), indicating that the activity of Hb was greatly enhanced and benzoyl moiety might change to the enol-form when forming a complex with Al3+, which was well accepted for some acylamides [52]. Therefore, for the benzoyl moiety in SQ-BH, in the case of coordinating with Al3+ in an enol-form, the two small π systems could merge into an extended conjugated π system with perfect planarity, thus giving the strong fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 1. In addition, some new peaks appeared along with the formation of the SQ-BH-Al3+ complex, which could be ascribed to signals of active hydrogens of OH and H2O containing species resulting from the hydrolysis of hydrated Al(III) salts [45,53]. With the further addition of Al3+ (>1.0 eq.), the signals of protons in SQ-BH remained almost the same, which confirmed 1:1 complexation between SQ-BH and Al3+ (Fig. 6e).

The reversibility of SQ-BH was observed by a reversible binding experiment since reversibility was an additional significant advantage for a practical chemosensor. As shown in Fig. 7 , when a little excess of EDTA was added to a solution containing SQ-BH and Al3+, the fluorescence signal centered at 505 nm due to the binding of Al3+ to SQ-BH was dramatically diminished, indicating the restoration of the free SQ-BH. However, with the addition of additional Al3+, the fluorescence quenched could be almost recovered again. The experimental results showed the process of titrating SQ-BH with Al3+ was reversible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation for SQ-BH and SQ-BH-Al3+ complex
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the sensing mechanism of SQ-BH towards Al3+, DFT and TD-DFT were applied to the calculations on SQ-BH and SQ-BH-Al3+ complex in the ground state (S0) and the excited states (S1) on the basis of the information provided by Job plot, ESI-mass and 1H NMR titration.

SQ-BH emitted a weak fluorescence in MeOH-H2O (9:1) solution (10 mM HEPES buffer at pH 7.0) at ambient temperature, which might be attributed to PET [39,46,47] or ICT [49,50] according to the results of experiments. To determine whether PET or ICT processes for SQ-BH would occur at ambient temperature, the analysis of the frontier molecular orbitals (FAO) of SQ-BH and SQ-BH-Al3+ complex were given. It could be seen from Fig. 8 that the HOMO and LUMO of both SQ-BH and SQ-BH-Al3+ were obvious π and π* characteristics, respectively. In the case of SQ-BH, the major molecular orbital contributions was determined for the HOMO → LUMO transition (459 nm, Table S1), which displayed an obvious intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) from the aniline moiety to the benzoyl moiety during the course of transition. For the SQ-BH-Al3+ complex, the major molecular orbital contributions were also determined for the HOMO → LUMO transition (498 nm, Table S1), which showed a similar but more significant ICT process comparing with SQ-BH. It indicated that the extension of the conjugation with more perfect planity and greater probability of ICT after the coordination of SQ-BH with Al3+. Moreover, comparing the LUMO between SQ-BH and SQ-BH-Al3+, the charge density of nitrogen atom of imine moiety greatly increased when Al3+ coordinated with SQ-BH, suggesting the PET process from nitrogen atom of imine moiety to the excited benzoyl moiety was blocked and then the fluorescence was switched “ON”. Therefore, the results of theoretical calculation showed it was the combined PET and ICT processes that weakened even quenched the fluorescence of SQ-BH, which was well agreed with the results of experiments.

Upon coordination of Al3+ with SQ-BH, the energy levels of both the LUMO and HOMO became lower than those of free SQ-BH, indicating the easiness of conversion from SQ-BH to SQ-BH-Al3+ and the extra stability of SQ-BH-Al3+. At the same time, the decrease in energy level of the LUMO was more significant than that of the HOMO and then led to the narrowing of the energy gap between the LUMO and HOMO of SQ-BH-Al3+ comparing to SQ-BH. Seen from Fig. 8, the energy gap between the LUMO and HOMO of SQ-BH-Al3+ (2.83 ev) was 0.21ev lower than that of SQ-BH (2.62 ev) and the tendency of ICT process was more significant than that of SQ-BH, illustrating that the coordination of SQ-BH with Al3+ promoted the ICT effect from the aniline moiety to the benzoyl moiety by increasing the electron-donating ability of aniline moiety as donor part and the electron-withdrawing ability of benzoyl moiety as accepter and thus leading to the strengthened “push –pull” effects. Therefore, a red shift in the emission spectra was observed upon the coordination of SQ-BH to Al3+.

In summary, the calculation results showed that the sensing mechanism of SQ-BH towards Al3+ resulted from the large and rigid conjugated π systems of SQ-BH-Al3+, which inhibited the non-radiative PET process and caused a large chealation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) effect. And the observation of a red shift in emission was ascribed to the enhanced ICT effect. Therefore, the CHEF effect and the inhibition of PET played a great role in fluorescence enhancement [1,7,8,39,46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell toxicity of SQ-BH and its bioimaging with Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of a sensor to image and even determine chemical or biological species in living cells is of great significance and deserves attention [30,35,36,54–59]. Thanks to the favorable spectroscopic properties of SQ-BH, the potential value of SQ-BH applied in biological fields was explored. The cellular toxicity of SQ-BH to HUVEC cells in MeOH/H2O was evaluated by a standard MTT assay (Fig. 9 ). Although the cellular viabilities decreased in varying degrees with the increase of concentrations (10–50 μM) of SQ-BH incubated with HUVEC cells for 12 and 24 h, the cellular viabilities (24 h) even highly reached up to 102% in the presence of 10 μM SQ-BH (a concentration used for cell imaging), indicating the excellent biocompatibility of SQ-BH and its low toxicity for HUVEC cells.

Then SQ-BH was used for imaging in HUVEC cells to detect Al3+ in living cells. Towards this end, HUVEC cells were first incubated with SQ-BH (10 μM) for 30 min and then treated with Al(NO3)3 (30 μM) for another 30 min. As shown in Fig. 10 , the cells stained only with SQ-BH (10 μM) exhibited weak intracellular fluorescence (Fig. 10a), whereas a significant intracellular fluorescence enhancement was observed when both SQ-BH (10 μM) and Al3+ (30 μM) were uptaken by the living cells (Fig. 10b), indicating that SQ-BH was permeable to HUVEC cells and bonded intracellular Al3+. Bright-field image of HUVEC cells treated with SQ-BH and Al3+ showed that the HUVEC cells were with good morphology and biocompatible with SQ-BH throughout the imaging experiments (Fig. 10c). In addition, the overlay image of confocal fluorescence and brightfield of HUVEC cells treated with SQ-BH and Al3+ demonstrated that the fluorescence signals were mainly localized in the perinuclear area (Fig. 10d), suggesting the subcellular distribution of Al3+. These results implied that SQ-BH were cellmembrane-permeable and might be used for monitoring and detecting intracellular Al3+ in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with other chemosensor for Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Obviously, squaraine-based chemosensor SQ-BH can be used for the detection of Al3+ in both aqueous media and live cells. However, numerous fluorescent sensors are known for Al3+, therefore, it is necessary to compare SQ-BH to other chemosensors used for the detection of Al3+. As shown in Table 1 , a clear comparison with some reported sensors for the detection of Al3+ was presented. By contrast, chemosensor SQ-BH exhibited the unique chemical structure, lower detection limit, wider applicable pH range and cellular imaging.

In conclusion, we have designed and developed a novel and simple squaraine-based “turn on” fluorescent chemosensor SQ-BH with ICT character. And SQ-BH can be used for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Al3+ in methanol-water buffer solution and in living cells. The results of experiments and theoretical calculation showed that the sensing mechanism of SQ-BH was associated with PET, CHEFF and ICT. It is worth noting that the combination of PET and ICT mechanisms in designing a chemosensor will be meaningful, since the fluorescence enhancement accompanying with emission wavelength shifted can achieve higher signal to noise ratio and then efficiently amplify the recognition event. At the same time, cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging experiment have shown that SQ-BH has excellent biocompatibility and can be used to monitor Al3+ in living cells and map its subcellular distribution. We believe this facile way to detect Al3+ will contribute to the potential applications in related fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21363014, 21103082), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province of China (No. 20142BCB23003, 20143ACB21021), and the Key Science and Technology Program of Education Department of Jiangxi Province (No. GJJ170945).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117590.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of an acylhydrazone based fluorescence probe for Al3+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. J. Liu, F. F. Tian, X. Y. Fan, F. L. Jiang, Y. Liu, Fabrication of an acylhydrazone based fluorescence probe for Al3+, Sens. Actuators, B 240 (2017) 916–925.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective chemosensor for Al(III) and Zn(II) and its coordination with metal ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Cao, X. J. Zheng, J. P. Sun, W. T. Wong, D. C. Fang, J. X. Zhang, L. P. Jin, A highly selective chemosensor for Al(III) and Zn(II) and its coordination with metal ions, Inorg. Chem. 53 (2014) 3012–3021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of highly sensitive, red-emitting, fluorogenic dye for microviscosity and mitochondrial imaging in embryonic stem cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. O. Raja, G. Sivaraman, A. Mukherjee, C. Duraisamy, A. Gulyani, Facile synthesis of highly sensitive, red-emitting, fluorogenic dye for microviscosity and mitochondrial imaging in embryonic stem cells, Chemistryselect 2(2017) 4609-4616.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boranil dye based “turn-on” fluorescent probes for detection of hydrogen peroxide and their cell imaging application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Shanmugapriya, K. Rajaguru, G. Sivaraman, S. Muthusubramanian, N. Bhuvanesh, Boranil dye based “turn-on” fluorescent probes for detection of hydrogen peroxide and their cell imaging application, RSC Adv. 6(2016) 85838-85843.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diverse benzothiazole based chemodosimeters for the detection of cyanide in aqueous media and in HeLa cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Vidya, M. Iniya, G. Sivaraman, R. V. Sumesh, D. chellappa, Diverse benzothiazole based chemodosimeters for the detection of cyanide in aqueous media and in HeLa cells, Sens. Actuators, B 242 (2017) 434-442.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rhodamine based effective chemosensor for Chromium(III) and their application in live cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Sunnapu, N. G. Kotla, B. Maddiboyina, G. S. Asthana, J. Shanmugapriya, K. Sekar, S. Singaravadivel, G. Sivaraman, Rhodamine based effective chemosensor for Chromium(III) and their application in live cell imaging, Sens. Actuators, B 246 (2017) 761-768.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pineapple peel-derived carbon dots: applications as sensor, molecular keypad lock, and memory device

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. A. A. Vandarkuzhali, S. Natarajan, S. Jeyabalan, G. Sivaraman, S. Singaravadivel, S. Muthusubramanian, B. Viswanathan, Pineapple peel-derived carbon dots: applications as sensor, molecular keypad lock, and memory device, ACS Omega 3 (2018), 12584-12592.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient “Ratiometric” fluorescent chemosensor for the selective detection of Hg2+ ions based on phosphonates: its live cell imaging and molecular keypad lock applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. G. V. Kumar, R. S. Kannan, T. C.-K. Yang, J. Rajesh, G. Sivaraman, An efficient “Ratiometric” fluorescent chemosensor for the selective detection of Hg2+ ions based on phosphonates: its live cell imaging and molecular keypad lock applications, Anal. Methods 9(2019) 901-916.
